coredata.dc:title: Laser fabricated carbon quantum dots in anti-solvent for highly efficient carbon-based perovskite solar cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shuhan
Li, Yang
Liu, Ke
Chen, Mengwei
Peng, Weidong
Yang, Yingping
Li, Xiangyou
coredata.dc:description: Additive passivation can be an effective strategy to regulate and control the properties of organic–inorganic halide perovskite film. In this article, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), fabricated by non-focused laser irradiation of carbon nanomaterial diluted in anti-solvent ethyl acetate, denoted as EACQDs, were adopted for perovskite film defect passivation and modification of carbon-based CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite solar cells (PSCs). The size of EACQDs can be tuned by manipulating the laser fluence. The morphology of perovskite film was uncovered through scanning electron microscopy and atomic force microscopy. After embedding of EACQDs, the defect in perovskite crystal was reduced, resulting in the decreased carrier recombination and accelerated carrier transportation, which were demonstrated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, photoluminescence and time-resolved photoluminescence. As a consequence, with the optimization of 0.01 mg/mL EACQDs (1064 nm–300 mJ·pulse−1·cm−2–10 min), the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of carbon-based PSCs achieved a maximum value of 16.43%, which improved 23.81% when compared with the pristine PSCs of 13.27%. Furthermore, the EACQDs optimized PSCs also exhibited an excellent stability and still retained 86% of its initial PCE after 50-day storage at the room atmosphere with a humidity of 30–50%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Non-focused laser irradiation
Anti-solvent
Defect passivation
Carbon-based perovskite solar cells

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, 11874167), and the Department of Science and Technology of Guangdong Province (2020B1212060067). The authors thank the Analytical and Testing Center of Huazhong University of Science and Technology for sample characterization and data measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic preparation process of laser generated EACQDs. (b) CN colloidal solution before and after laser irradiation. (c) TEM image and (d) Size distribution of EACQDs. The inset depicts lattice spacing. (e) XPS spectra of EACQDs and (f) deconvolution of C.
FTIR spectra of (a) laser generated EACQDs and (b) EACQDs, MAPbI3 and EACQDs-MAPbI3. (c) XRD image of pristine and 0.01EACQDs optimized PSCs. (d) XPS spectra of MAPbI3 and EACQDs-MAPbI3.

SEM images of (a) Pristine and (b) 0.01EACQDs optimized perovskite films. (c) cross-sectional image of EACQDs optimized PSCs (not include carbon electrode). AFM images of (d) Pristine and (e) 0.01EACQDs optimized perovskite films. (f) UV–vis spectra of pristine and 0.01EACQDs optimized PSCs.

(a) J-V curves of PSCs optimized by different concentrations of EACQDs. (b) IPCE spectra of pristine and 0.01EACQDs optimized PSCs. (c) PL and (d) TR-PL spectra of pristine and 0.01EACQDs optimized perovskite films.

(a) Dark J-V curves of pristine and 0.01EACQDs optimized PSCs. (b) Dark I-V curves of pristine and 0.01EACQDs optimized perovskite films. (c) EIS spectra. (d) J-V curves with different scan directions. (e) and (f) Light intensity dependent current density and voltage of pristine and 0.01EACQDs optimized PSCs.

(a) XRD spectra of pristine and 0.01EACQDs optimized perovskite films stored in the air under 30–50% humidity for 7 days. (b) Normalized PCEs of pristine and 0.01EACQDs optimized PSCs aged in the room temperature with a humidity of 30–50% in the dark.
Photovoltaic parameters of the devices based on the pristine and optimized perovskite films with different concentrations of EACQDs.

head.ce:title: Laser fabricated carbon quantum dots in anti-solvent for highly efficient carbon-based perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, 1037 Luoyu Road, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, 1037 Luoyu Road, Wuhan 430074, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, School of Science, Wuhan University of Technology, 122 Luoshi Road, Wuhan 430070, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, School of Science, Wuhan University of Technology, 122 Luoshi Road, Wuhan 430070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Additive passivation can be an effective strategy to regulate and control the properties of organic–inorganic halide perovskite film. In this article, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), fabricated by non-focused laser irradiation of carbon nanomaterial diluted in anti-solvent ethyl acetate, denoted as EACQDs, were adopted for perovskite film defect passivation and modification of carbon-based CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite solar cells (PSCs). The size of EACQDs can be tuned by manipulating the laser fluence. The morphology of perovskite film was uncovered through scanning electron microscopy and atomic force microscopy. After embedding of EACQDs, the defect in perovskite crystal was reduced, resulting in the decreased carrier recombination and accelerated carrier transportation, which were demonstrated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, photoluminescence and time-resolved photoluminescence. As a consequence, with the optimization of 0.01 mg/mL EACQDs (1064 nm–300 mJ·pulse−1·cm−2–10 min), the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of carbon-based PSCs achieved a maximum value of 16.43%, which improved 23.81% when compared with the pristine PSCs of 13.27%. Furthermore, the EACQDs optimized PSCs also exhibited an excellent stability and still retained 86% of its initial PCE after 50-day storage at the room atmosphere with a humidity of 30–50%.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, as a new efficient absorber material, perovskite material has been widely investigated in perovskite solar cells (PSCs), which result in the drastically increasement in power conversion efficiency (PCE) up to 25.2% in decade [1–5]. However, in spite of the superior photoelectric properties including high light harvesting efficiency, fast carrier transport speed as well as long carrier diffusion length [6]. The performance of PSCs can be influenced by several unsolved issues and of which the quality of perovskite film is the dominant factor. In order to fabricate perovskite film with remarkable properties, multiple approaches including anti-solvents engineering, preparation way and additive modification, have been employed, aiming at passivating defects at the interface and in the grain boundaries (GBs) of perovskite film [7–11].

Additive modification has been extensively exploited for defect passivation in perovskite film and promoting performance of PSCs [7,11–13]. Among them, nanoparticle additives can manipulate perovskite nucleation formation process by defect passivation at surface and along GBs, which will result in a protective layer to restrain the interaction between oxygen, moisture in the air and perovskite, thus leading to an improved stability and photoelectric efficiency of PSCs. Liu et al. utilized two-dimensional graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots to optimize PSCs as well as photodetectors, accomplishing high performance PSCs and PDs [13]. Han et al. incorporated carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in perovskite film to accelerate charge transport and reduce charge recombination, giving rise to a 24.3% improvement in PCE and excellent ambient stability when compared to the pristine PSCs [14]. Wang et al. introduced silicon quantum dots by laser irradiation to enhance a broadband light harvesting efficiency and a 5.6% improvement in PCE of PSCs [15]. The functional groups at the surface of CQDs can interact with the uncoordinated Pb ions, which will passivate defect in perovskite film and thus improve PCE of PSCs. On account of this, Ma et al. reported CQDs prepared by acetone and embedded in perovskite film to passivate defect. After the embedding of CQDs, the defect in perovskite film was passivated, which fostered the charge transport, reduced the charge recombination, and eventually obtained an increased PCE and improved stability of PSCs [16]. The above investigation research indicated that appropriate incorporation of nanoparticle additives can effectively contribute to the defect passivation, crystallization of perovskite film and performance enhancement of PSCs.

As a nanoparticle additive with fascinating photoluminescence (PL), CQDs possess manifold properties including extensively PL output, supreme moisture resistance ability as well as low cytotoxicity [17–20], which have various applications such as light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and security ink [21,22]. Whereas, there are many preparation methods for CQDs, most of which are about chemical reaction. However, the ineliminable redundant residual chemical ligands during reaction will affect the deployment of surface functional groups on CQDs. Moreover, the relatively long synthesis time was unsuitable for large-scale preparation of CQDs. Thus, a new fabrication way was needed to solve the above problems. Laser synthesis of colloidal particles was found to be a convenient and fast way for their preparation, which has rapid development in the past decades [23–25]. In fact, laser induced CQDs have been generated by non-focused laser irradiation in various anti-solvents such as methylbenzene (MB), chlorobenzene (CB) and hexafluorobenzene (HB) [26–28]. The CQDs can be generated within several minutes and the sizes were adjustable with the change of laser fluence, which was deeply rapid and convenient. Herein, motivated by the merit of laser fabrication of colloidal particles and the universally anti-solvent method for perovskite film preparation, a new way for the fabrication of PSCs with defect passivated high-quality perovskite film seems rational and available.

In this article, CQDs were prepared by non-focused laser irradiation in carbon nanomaterial (CN) containing anti-solvent ethyl acetate (EA), marked as EACQDs, which have diverse morphologies and tunable sizes based on different laser fluence. The wavelength dependent PL emission phenomenon of EACQDs can be observed with the excitation wavelength increasing from 300 to 500 nm. The functional groups and chemical bonding information of EACQDs were revealed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). To investigate the morphologies of perovskite films before and after EACQDs optimization, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) as well as atomic force microscopy (AFM) were used. After EACQDs incorporation, the fast quenching of steady-state PL and reduced charge lifetime indicated the passivated defects and accelerated charge transportation. Furthermore, the EACQDs optimization of PSCs resulted in a significant improvement of fill factor (FF, 67.47%) and PCE (16.43%) when compared to the pristine PSCs of 58.21% and 13.27%, respectively. Moreover, the stability of EACQDs optimized PSCs was enhanced when stored in the air at the humidity of 30–50% for 50 days. As far as we are concerned, few works have been focused on the laser synthesis of CQDs in anti-solvent that applied to carbon-electrode based, hole-conductor-free mesoporous PSCs. Therefore, it is consequential to thoroughly elucidate the potential mechanism of performance enhancement in EACQDs optimized PSCs. Besides, this work can pave a way for the widely applications of laser fabricated nanoparticles not only CQDs in nontoxic anti-solvent such as EA.

Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO, 7 Ω−1) conductive glass substrates were bought from Advanced Election Technology CO,.Ltd. Titanium (diisopropoxide) bis (2,4-pentanedionate) (TIPD, 75%) isopropanol solution, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, >99.5%) as well as N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, >99.5%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Carbon nanomaterial (CN, 30 nm, 99.5%) were purchased form Aladdin. Ethyl acetate (EA, >99.5%) was traded from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Limited Corporation. Titanium dioxide (TiO2, 30 NRT-D, 20% ~wt) was acquired from Dyesol. Zirconium dioxide (ZrO2, 30 NRT-D, 20% ~wt) and carbon paste were bought from Shanghai MaterWin New Materials Corporation. Finally, lead (II) iodide (PbI2, 99.99%) and methylamine iodide (CH3NH3I, 99.9%) were bought from Xi’an Polymer Light Technology Corporation.

5 mg CNs were added into 100 mL EA in a plastic bottle and dispersed by ultrasonic vibration for 10 min. Then, 4 mL solution was taken and infused into a glass bottle, irradiated by a non-focused laser beam (Quantel brilliant B, wavelength: 1064 nm, repetition rate: 10 Hz, pulse width: 10 ns, beam diameter: 9 mm) for 10 min. In the irradiation process, a magnetic stirrer was adopted to maintain the uniform solution. Besides, the laser fluence varied from 100 to 300 mJ·pulse−1·cm−2.

The FTO conductive glass was laved by ultrasonic vibration for 30 min in the order of detergent, acetone, isopropanol, and ethanol, separately. After cleaning, ozone plasma was utilized for the organic residue elimination on the surface of FTO for 20 min. For the TiO2 compact (c-TiO2) layer preparation, the precursor solution (TIPD: ethanol = 1: 19) was spin-coated on the FTO substrate at 4000 rpm for 20 s, followed by 500 °C and 30 min annealing. As it cooled down, TiO2 mesoporous (m-TiO2) layer was formed by spin-coating TiO2 paste (TiO2: ethanol = 1: 5) at 3500 rpm for 20 s, then annealed for 30 min at 500 °C. Subsequently, ZrO2 layer was fabricated via spin-coating (5000 rpm, 20 s) ZrO2 paste (ZrO2: ethanol = 1: 5) with 500 °C annealing temperature for 30 min. Afterwards, ozone plasma (20 min treatment) was adopted for the enhancement of wettability on the ZrO2 surface before perovskite film formation. Then, CH3NH3PbI3 (MAPbI3) layer was prepared by spin-coating 30 μL perovskite precursor for 1000 rpm, 10 s and 4000 rpm, 20 s. At the second process, 200 μL anti-solvent with different concentrations of EACQDs (0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.02 and 0.05 mg/mL, marked as pristine, 0.005EACQDs, 0.01EACQDs, 0.02EACQDs and 0.05EACQDs), was dropped immediately on the perovskite film surface 10 s before the end of the process, which was heated at 100 °C for 10 min on a heating platform to form an uniform surface. Eventually, 10 μm carbon paste was screen-printed on perovskite film surface and annealed at 100 °C for 10 min to obtain the whole PSC.

Ultraviolet–visible spectra (UV–vis, Shimadzu, UV-3600) was utilized to characterize pristine and EACQDs optimized PSCs. Steady-state PL spectra was surveyed by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimazu, RF-6000) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TR-PL) was measured by a FLS 980E fluorometer. FTIR spectra was done by a fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Bruker, VERTEX 70). The crystal structure of perovskite was determined by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, PANalytical, PW3040/60) with the Cu-Kα line (λ = 0.154443 nm) excitation source at 40 KV and 40 mA. XPS (Thermo Fisher Scientific, ESCALAB 250Xi) measurements was revealed by a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source at normal emission. FESEM (FEI, Nova NanoSEM 450) as well as high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, FEI, Talos F200X) was adopted to characterize the morphology of EACQDs and PSCs. To disclose the different surface roughness between pristine and EACQDs optimized perovskite film, AFM (Shimadzu, SPM9700) was employed. Keithley 2400 source meter and solar simulator (Oriel, model 91192-1000) were exploited to record current density–voltage (J-V) curves of the pristine and EACQDs optimized PSCs with the active area of 0.06 cm2 under one sun AM 1.5G (100 mW cm−2) illumination. Incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectrum was characterized via a 150 W xenon lamp (Oriel) fitted with a monochromator (Cornerstone 74004). The charge transportation property was demonstrated by electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using the electrochemical workstation (Zahner, Kronach) with the frequency ranging from 10 mHz to 10 MHz under AM 1.5G sunlight illumination as well as a bias voltage of 0.9 V. For the PSCs stability test, the pristine and EACQDs optimized PSCs were stored under ambient air indoor at a humidity of 30–50% for 50 days. In addition, thermal gravity analysis (TGA, PerkinElmer, Diamond TG/DTA) was performed at a steady heating rate of 20 °C min−1 under N2 gas flow and the research temperature arranged from 30 to 600 °C.

The detailed fabrication process of EACQDs was shown in Fig. 1 a. Typically, a certain amount of colloidal solution was injected into a glass bottle to be laser irradiated with magnetic stirring to keep the solution uniform. After laser irradiation, the colloidal solution changed from dark to light yellow, as displayed in Fig. 1b. Moreover, the resulted light-yellow solution exhibited a PL phenomenon, depending on the excitation wavelength (Fig. S1a). With the excitation wavelength increasing, the PL emission peak red-shifted, which is a quintessential characteristic of CQDs [29]. To further confirm the obtained EACQDs, Raman spectrum was employed. As presented in Fig. S1b, the D and G bands of CQDs are found to be located at 1366 and 1588 cm−1. Besides, from the TEM image of the resulted EACQDs in Fig. 1c, it is apparent that the lattice spacing is 0.213 nm, in line with the (100) lattice fringe of graphite [30] and the average size of CQDs is 5.37 nm. As depicted in Fig. S1c, the FTIR spectra of carbon-containing colloidal solution before and after laser irradiation appear the same, implying the unchanged properties of EA. In order to exclude the PL emission derived from EA, the laser irradiated solution was dried to obtain EACQDs, then refilled with ethanol and the emergence of visible PL indicated that the PL was originated from EACQDs (Fig. S1d). For non-focused laser irradiation, it is convenient to precisely manipulate the size of EACQDs. As shown in Fig. S2, raw CN has an average size of about 30 nm. However, when the laser fluence increased to 100 mJ·pulse−1·cm−2, the carbon material represented an obscure morphology, which can be attributed to the intermediate phase during laser irradiation. With a further increase of the laser fluence from 150 to 300 mJ·pulse−1·cm−2, the size of the obtained EACQDs ranged from 2.65 nm to 5.37 nm, demonstrating the laser fluence dependent phenomenon of EACQDs size, which has been observed in many laser-irradiated materials [31–34]. XPS was utilized to reveal the surface chemistry of EACQDs, as shown in Fig. 1e. C 1 s and O 1 s were found in the whole spectrum of EACQDs. The deconvolution of C was conducted and displayed in Fig. 1f. The peaks located at about 284.8, 285.8, 286.3 and 288.8 eV can be assigned to CC, CC, CO and CO, separately [35–38].

For the purpose of ascertaining chemical bonding at the surface of EACQDs, FTIR spectrum was shown in Fig. 2 a, further verifying the existence of CO and CC that were perceived at about 1690 cm−1 and 1660 cm−1 [39]. Whereas CH and CH2 group have strong stretching vibrations located near 3000 and 1480 cm−1, the weak COH peak also can be seen around 3450 cm−1 [40]. The above results offer a conspicuous clew to comprehend the surface functional groups peculiarity of EACQDs especially the hydrophobic property. which will lead to an auspicious stability of PSCs [14,39]. To reveal the interaction mechanism of EACQDs and perovskite, FTIR spectra of EACQDs and EACQDs optimized perovskite film were researched in comparison in Fig. 2b. The shift of CO peak to a lower frequency range was induced by the transference of electron clouds from CO to PbO, indicating the interaction of intermolecular between CO bonds with Pb2+ [41,42]. In terms of the formation and the surface oxygen abundant carboxylate groups of EACQDs, a mechanism for non-focused laser irradiation of EACQDs was proposed, as depicted in Fig. S3. The non-focused pulsed laser will induce a spatially localized high temperature and pressure in EA, heating the CNs to a plasma state, followed by the process of carbon plasma condensation immediately after every laser pulse, which lead to the formation of EACQDs [43]. During laser irradiation, the surface of EACQDs can be oxidized by the exploded or residual oxygen atoms in EA and interact with carbon plasma, resulting in the carboxylate groups formation on the EACQDs’ surface [44,45]. XRD results of pristine and EACQDs optimized PSCs are shown in Fig. 2c and the immobile position of the diffraction peaks prove that the incorporation of EACQDs has neglectable influence on the perovskite crystal. Moreover, the three remarkable peaks can be attributed to the standard MAPbI3 with the crystal plane of (110), (220) and (330). As described in Fig. 2d, the two peaks located at 138.0 and 142.8 eV are ascribed to Pb 4f7/2 and Pb 4f5/2 of pristine PSCs. In addition, the two tiny peaks at about 136.0 and 141.3 eV can be put down to the metallic lead, indicating the minor iodine vacancy, which will act as the non-radiative recombination center and deteriorate the photoelectric performance of PSCs [46]. However, after the optimization of EACQDs, the two metallic lead peaks eliminated as a consequence of the passivation effect of EACQDs [46,47].

The SEM morphologies of pristine and 0.01EACQDs optimized PSCs were shown in Fig. 3 a and b. It was manifest that the perovskite film surface became smoother after EACQDs optimization. Besides, the SEM images of perovskite film optimized by different concentrations of EACQDs were displayed in Fig. S4. The structure of the device was Glass/FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/ZrO2/EACQDs-MAPbI3, which can be revealed by cross-sectional SEM image presented in Fig. 3c. The c-TiO2 and m-TiO2 layer own a thickness of about 250 nm. In addition, the thickness of ZrO2/EACQDs-MAPbI3 layer is about 750 nm. To confirm the surface morphologies of pristine and 0.01EACQDs optimized perovskite film, AFM images were employed in Fig. 3d and e. After 0.01EACQDs optimization, the surface roughness decreased from 78.1 to 51.8 nm, in accordance with the above SEM images, indicating a preferably surface morphology, which was beneficial to charge transportation. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 3f, UV–vis spectrum of 0.01EACQDs optimized PSCs exhibited a little bit higher in the whole visible light region than pristine PSCs, which can be ascribed to the enhancement of perovskite crystallinity.

The J-V photovoltaic curves and plots of parameters were presented in Fig. 4 a and Fig. S5. From the above results, it was obvious that after EACQDs optimization, Voc, Jsc and especially FF of the PSCs had a significant improvement, resulting in the notable promotion of PCE and achieved the maximum value of 16.43% with 0.01EACQDs optimization, superior to the pristine PSCs (13.27%). Moreover, the corresponding parameters of 30 individual devices were listed in Table 1 . While the average parameter values of Voc, Jsc, FF and PCE for 0.01EACQDs optimized PSCs were 1.021 ± 0.005 V, 22.79 ± 0.69 mA/cm2, 65.29 ± 1.31% and 15.14 ± 0.51%, respectively, which have dramatical enhancement when compared with the pristine PSCs (1.005 ± 0.012 V, 21.65 ± 0.49 mA/cm2, 55.86 ± 1.33% and 12.15 ± 0.49%). Furthermore, IPCE spectra of pristine and 0.01EACQDs were shown in Fig. 4b, demonstrating a whole visible light region increasement. Thereafter, the integrated Jsc are 21.06 and 22.12 mA/cm2 for pristine and 0.01EACQDs optimized PSCs, which are consistent with the obtained Jsc value. To figure out the difference between charge extraction and transportation in pristine and 0.01EACQDs optimized perovskite film, PL and TR-PL were utilized by spin-coating perovskite precursor solution on the FTO substrate. As shown in Fig. 4c, the apparently lower intensity for the 0.01EACQDs perovskite film represented the fast quenching effect, implying the accelerated charge extraction at the interface of FTO/EACQDs-MAPbI3, induced by the defect passivation that decreased the defect density and charge recombination. TR-PL spectra can further prove the above analysis, as presented in Fig. 4d and the parameters were listed in Table S1. The average carrier lifetime of EACQDs optimized perovskite film was 106.33 ns, shorter than the pristine sample (130.03 ns), indicating a fast charge transportation, in accord with PL results. Thus, the improvement of current density can be obtained by EACQDs optimization.

The dark J-V results were used to analyze the influence of EACQDs additive embedding in perovskite film for the recombination of carriers, presented in Fig. 5 a. The lower leakage current density of EACQDs optimized PSCs demonstrated the hindered current leakage and the decreased carrier recombination rate after EACQDs incorporation due to the high-quality perovskite film [13]. To investigate defect density of pristine and EACQDs optimized perovskite film, the space charge limited current (SCLC) in the dark mode was conducted with a structure of FTO/Perovskite/C in Fig. 5b and the defect density can be calculated by the equation below [48].
(1)
N
t r a p
=
2
ε 0
ε r
V
T F L
q
L
2

In the above equation, Ntrap is the density of the defects, q is the electron charge, L is the thickness of perovskite film, ε0 is the vacuum permittivity and the εr of MAPbI3 is 32 [49]. From Fig. 5b, the trap-filling limited voltage (VTFL) of pristine and 0.01EACQDs optimized perovskite films were obtained to be 1.033 and 0.939 V. Therefore, the calculated value of Ntrap were 6.50 × 1015 cm−3 and 5.91 × 1015 cm−3, respectively. The distinct reduced Ntrap of EACQDs optimized perovskite film verified the decreasing result of defects via EACQDs incorporation, which was attributed to the defect passivation of EACQDs that suppressed charge recombination and promoted charge transportation, consistence with the increased PL quenching and decreased charge lifetime. To further probe the resistance in PSCs, EIS was adopted as shown in Fig. 5c and the corresponding parameters were fitted and concluded in Table S2. The Nyquist plots have two semicircles including the left high frequency region corresponding to the recombination resistance (Rrec) and the right high frequency region corresponding to the transfer resistance (Rtr) [50,51]. It was found that the value of Rrec and Rtr for pristine and EACQDs optimized PSCs were 494 Ω, 109.9 Ω and 519.7 Ω, 102.3 Ω. The increased Rrec and deceased Rtr indicated the suppressed charge recombination and accelerated charge transportation.

The hysteresis behavior of pristine and EACQDs optimized PSCs were researched in Fig. 5d and the corresponding parameters were listed in Table S3. Whereas, the hysteresis index can be evaluated by the equation [52,53].
(2)
h =
P C E
r e v e r s e
-
P C E
f o r w a r d
/
P C E
r e v e r s e

After calculation, the hysteresis index of pristine and EACQDs optimized PSCs were 12.76% and 6.02%. The significant diminution of the index testified the restrained hysteresis, implying the superior performance with EACQDs incorporation. Moreover, the recombination losses were further studied by light power dependent J-V. As depicted in Fig. 5e and f, the light intensities (P) varied from 5 to 100 mW/cm2 and the tiptop linear relationship between P and Jsc as well as P and Voc implied the excellent properties of PSCs. For the short circuit mode, the internal voltage will result in the movement of electrons and holes to electrodes before their recombination, which can be uncovered by the equation
J
sc
∝
P
∝
[54], where α is close to 1 in spite of the device type. As shown in Fig. 5e, the fitted α value of EACQDs optimized PSCs is 0.989, closer to 1 than the pristine device, demonstrating the insignificant space charge limit in EACQDs optimized PSCs. For the open circuit mode, the electrons and holes generated by the light irradiation will recombine entirely. Furthermore, the recombination mechanism involved in the process, can be revealed by
V
oc
∝ n K T / q ln
( P )
[34]. K, T and q represent the constant of Boltzmann, Kelvin temperature and elementary charge, separately. If the coefficient n is close to 1, the bimolecular recombination will become dominant. On the contrary, if n is greater than 1, the defect-auxiliary Shockley-Read-Hall (SRH) recombination will appear [55]. In Fig. 5f, n decreased from 1.158 to 1.023 after 0.01EACQDs incorporation, indicating the SRH recombination in PSCs was inhibited. Meanwhile, the decreased SRH recombination was ascribed to the accelerated charge transportation because of the promoted perovskite surface as well as the defect passivation effect arising out of EACQDs. Thus, from the above results, it is reasonable to conclude that the incorporation of EACQDs is believed to obtain a better surface modification and passivation which contributes to the performance enhancement of PSCs.

The stability of PSCs can be a severe issue for the large-scale applications, which was investigated in Fig. 6 . It is evident that after being preserved in the air for 7 days, the pristine device indicated a notable peak of PbI2. Nevertheless, the 0.01EACQDs optimized sample has a neglectable PbI2 peak at the same position, proving the passivation of EACQDs can retard the degradation of perovskite film due to the fact that hydrophobic functional groups on the surface of EACQDs can resist the moisture. Furthermore, Fig. 6b presented the normalized PCEs of pristine and EACQDs optimized PSCs aged in the air at room temperature under a humidity of 30–50%. After aging for 50 days, the 0.01EACQDs optimized PSCs can still maintain 86% of its initial PCE. While the pristine device can only keep 42% of the original PCE, a half lower than the 0.01EACQDs optimized sample, demonstrating the superior stability after 0.01EACQDs incorporation in the perovskite film. To investigate the thermal stability of the pristine and EACQDs optimized samples, TGA results were shown in Fig. S6. There are three main steps of the weight loss of the pristine perovskite powders at around 70 °C, 220 °C and 430 °C, which correspond to the sublimation of DMSO, CH3NH3I and PbI2, respectively [41,56]. It is obvious that after 0.01EACQDs optimization, the sublimation temperature of MAI and PbI2 are higher than the pristine perovskite sample, demonstrating that more energy is needed to break the interaction between 0.01EACQDs and perovskite precursors. In conclusion, the improvement in degradation after EACQDs incorporation can be attributed to the interaction between the surface hydrophobic functional groups such as CC, CO of EACQDs and uncoordinated Pb2+ in the perovskite film, which will reduce the defect density, accelerate the transfer of charges and decrease the charge recombination, eventually resulting in a splendid performance of PSCs.

The EACQDs optimized PSCs in this work have many difference and advantages when compared to other reported works. For the CQDs preparation methods, in this work, non-focused laser irradiation was utilized to fabricate CQDs with uniformity size deviation in the anti-solvent within few minutes. The experimental device is simple as shown in Fig. 1a and the laser irradiation is fast and convenient beyond the majority of the conventional chemical methods [39,57,58], which is very suitable for CQDs preparation. In addition, the fabrication process in the green anti-solvent EA can realize controllable ligand-free CQDs that is hard to fulfill in the conventional fabrication process. Furthermore, for the hole-conductor-free, carbon-based PSCs, the improvement and champion value of PCE is higher in this work than the reported work [39]. Although the PCE of PSCs in this work is still lower than the other CQDs modified PSCs based on metal electrode or even an additional hole-conductor layer [57,58], the lower cost as well as relatively excellent stability of EACQDs optimized PSCs can be a decent choice, which will pave a way for the low-cost, large-scale fabrication of PSCs in the future.

In this work, EACQDs were prepared via a facile method named non-focused laser irradiation in a CN containing vessel. The incorporation of EACQDs in perovskite film resulted in a better morphology, outstanding charge transport properties and lessened charge recombination due to the defect passivation effect of EACQDs, with a higher PCE of 16.43%. Besides, the 0.01EACQDs optimized PSC can still retain 86% of its original PCE after aging in the dark at a room temperature with a humidity of 30–50% for 50 days. This work also provides consequential comprehension of the EACQDs formation and perovskite defect passivation process, which is meaningful for the investigation of preeminent PSCs on the basis of laser matter interaction and optimization.

Shuhan Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yang Li: Investigation. Ke Liu: Supervision. Mengwei Chen: Investigation. Weidong Peng: Investigation. Yingping Yang: Supervision. Xiangyou Li: Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, 11874167), and the Department of Science and Technology of Guangdong Province (2020B1212060067). The authors thank the Analytical and Testing Center of Huazhong University of Science and Technology for sample characterization and data measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.05.034.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.Q. Liao, J.L. Duan, Y.Y. Zhao, Q.W. Tang, Toward fast charge extraction in all-inorganic CsPbBr3 perovskite solar cells by setting intermediate energy levels, Solar Energy 171 (2018) 279-285.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Passivation of the grain boundaries of CH3NH3PbI3 using carbon quantum dots for highly efficient perovskite solar cells with excellent environmental stability
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Guo, F.L Yuan, B. Zhang, S.J. Zhou, J. Zhang, Y.M. Bai, L.Z. Fan, T. Hayat, A. Alsaedi, Z.A. Tan, Passivation of the grain boundaries of CH3NH3PbI3 using carbon quantum dots for highly efficient perovskite solar cells with excellent environmental stability, 11 (2019) 115-124.
